---
title: "15 · The Spectral Theorem and Functional Analysis Preview"
subtitle: "Symmetric matrices, infinite dimensions, and the unification of linear algebra and analysis"
lesson-id: "t6.spectral-theorem"
tier: 6
prerequisites: ["t5.eigenvalues", "t5.real-analysis-foundation"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T5 L04 — Eigenvalues](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)
   and [T5 L15 — Real Analysis Foundation](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with eigenvalues, eigenvectors, diagonalisation?
3. Recall ε-δ continuity?
:::

## Touch

Pick a kind of matrix. The widget reports whether it has a complete
set of perpendicular eigenvectors — a clean axis system that
diagonalises it cleanly.

```{ojs}
viewof stSel = Inputs.select(["symmetric A: real eigenvalues + orthogonal eigenvectors", "complex Hermitian: same conclusion (Schur)", "PSD A: all eigenvalues ≥ 0", "infinite dim: Hilbert space generalisation"], {value: "symmetric A: real eigenvalues + orthogonal eigenvectors", label: "Statement:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(stSel) {
    case "symmetric A: real eigenvalues + orthogonal eigenvectors": info = "If A = A^T, then A = Q D Q^T where Q orthogonal (Q^T Q = I), D diagonal with real eigenvalues. EVERY symmetric matrix."; break;
    case "complex Hermitian: same conclusion (Schur)": info = "If A* = A (Hermitian, complex conjugate transpose equals itself), then A = U D U* with U unitary, D real diagonal."; break;
    case "PSD A: all eigenvalues ≥ 0": info = "Symmetric A is positive semi-definite iff x^T A x ≥ 0 for all x iff all eigenvalues ≥ 0. Foundation of convex optimisation."; break;
    case "infinite dim: Hilbert space generalisation": info = "Self-adjoint operators on Hilbert spaces have spectral decompositions involving spectral measures (Tier 7). Quantum mechanics' observables are self-adjoint."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:0.95rem; color:#c2185b; text-align:center;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

## Wonder

1. Why are real symmetric matrices so special?
2. **PCA, SVD, Fourier expansions** are all spectral decompositions
   in disguise. Why?
3. The infinite-dimensional generalisation: Hilbert spaces and
   self-adjoint operators (preview of Tier 7).

## Discover

### Problem 1

For symmetric $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$,
find eigenvalues and eigenvectors.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\det(A - \lambda I) = (2-\lambda)^2 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1,
3$.

For $\lambda = 1$: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} v = 0
\Rightarrow v_1 = -v_2$. Eigenvector $(1, -1)^T$.

For $\lambda = 3$: $v_1 = v_2$. Eigenvector $(1, 1)^T$.

**Note:** the two eigenvectors are *orthogonal*: $(1, -1) \cdot (1, 1)
= 0$. **No coincidence** — symmetric matrices always have orthogonal
eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues.
</details>

### Problem 2

The **spectral theorem (real)**: every symmetric matrix $A$ admits
$$A = Q D Q^T$$
where $Q$ is orthogonal ($Q^T Q = I$) and $D$ is diagonal with real
eigenvalues.

For $A$ from Problem 1: write the decomposition.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Normalised eigenvectors: $(1/\sqrt{2}, -1/\sqrt{2})^T$ and
$(1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2})^T$.

$Q = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$,
$D = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$.

Check: $Q D Q^T = A$. ✓

**Geometric meaning:** $A$ scales by 1 along $(1, -1)/\sqrt{2}$ and
by 3 along $(1, 1)/\sqrt{2}$. Pure stretching, no rotation, in the
orthogonal eigenbasis.
</details>

### Problem 3

**SVD (Singular Value Decomposition):** for *any* matrix $A$ (not
just symmetric), $A = U \Sigma V^T$ where $U, V$ orthogonal, $\Sigma$
diagonal with non-negative entries (singular values).

How does SVD generalise the spectral theorem?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**SVD applies to any matrix**, including non-symmetric and
rectangular. Eigenvalues of $A^T A$ are squared singular values.

For a symmetric $A$: SVD reduces (essentially) to spectral
decomposition.

**SVD is the workhorse of:**

- *PCA:* SVD of the data matrix.
- *Latent semantic analysis* (search, NLP).
- *Image compression:* keep only top-$k$ singular values.
- *Matrix completion* (Netflix prize): assume low-rank structure.

**Eckart-Young theorem:** truncated SVD gives the optimal low-rank
approximation in Frobenius norm.
</details>

### Problem 4

**PCA via SVD.** Centre data matrix $X$ ($n \times p$, $n$ samples,
$p$ features). Compute SVD: $X = U \Sigma V^T$. Columns of $V$ are
**principal components**.

Why?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Covariance matrix** $C = X^T X / n$ has eigenvectors = principal
components, eigenvalues = variances. Spectral decomposition of $C$ =
columns of $V$ from SVD of $X$.

**Top principal components** = directions of greatest variance =
columns of $V$ for largest singular values.

Used pervasively: data visualisation (project to top 2-3 PCs),
dimensionality reduction before clustering, denoising, feature
extraction.
</details>

### Problem 5

**Functional analysis preview.** Generalise: a **Hilbert space** is
an infinite-dim inner product space, complete (Cauchy sequences
converge).

Examples:

- $\ell^2$: square-summable sequences.
- $L^2[a, b]$: square-integrable functions on $[a, b]$.

A **self-adjoint operator** $T$ on a Hilbert space generalises a
symmetric matrix. The **spectral theorem** generalises:

$$T = \int_\sigma \lambda \, dE(\lambda)$$

(integral against a *spectral measure*).

Quantum mechanical observables are self-adjoint operators —
*eigenvalues are measured values*.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Eigenvalues of position operator: continuous spectrum (the real
line). Eigenvalues of energy of harmonic oscillator: discrete
($E_n = \hbar \omega (n + 1/2)$).**

The spectral theorem unifies:

- Linear algebra (finite dim).
- Fourier analysis (operators on $L^2$ — Fourier basis is the
  spectral decomposition of differentiation).
- Quantum mechanics (observables → spectra → measurement outcomes).

A genuine *unification* across mathematics.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**The spectral theorem closed a programme that took David Hilbert
the better part of a decade.** Between **1904 and 1910** Hilbert
published a series of six memoirs on integral equations and
"infinite-dimensional matrices" — the work that introduced
**Hilbert space**, *Eigenwert* (eigenvalue), and the spectral
decomposition of self-adjoint operators in his characteristic
slow-burning style. He proved the theorem for *bounded* operators;
**John von Neumann**, working out the new quantum mechanics in
1929–1932, extended it to *unbounded* operators (position and
momentum are unbounded), and put the whole apparatus on rigorous
ground in his *Mathematische Grundlagen der Quantenmechanik*. The
word *spectrum*, originally coined for the rainbow of frequencies
emitted by hot atoms, turned out to literally *be* the set of
eigenvalues of the atom's Hamiltonian — Hilbert's terminology
matched physics by happy accident, not by design.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
The spectral theorem is the statement that *symmetric matrices are
secretly diagonal* — in the right orthogonal frame, a symmetric
matrix is just stretching along perpendicular axes. SVD says
*every* matrix is secretly two such stretchings glued together. In
infinite dimensions, the same picture survives, just with
"sum over eigenvalues" replaced by "integral over a spectrum" — and
that one sentence is the entire mathematical foundation of quantum
mechanics.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Spectral theorem (real, finite-dim):** every real symmetric matrix
$A$ admits
$$A = Q D Q^T$$
with $Q$ orthogonal and $D$ diagonal with real eigenvalues.
**Equivalently:** $\mathbb{R}^n$ has an orthonormal basis of
eigenvectors of $A$.

**Hermitian (complex) version:** $A^* = A \Rightarrow A = U D U^*$.

**Positive (semi-)definite:** symmetric with all eigenvalues $> 0$
($\ge 0$).

**Singular value decomposition (SVD):** any $m \times n$ matrix
$A = U \Sigma V^T$ with $U \in O(m), V \in O(n)$, $\Sigma$
non-negative diagonal.

**Functional analysis (Tier 7 preview):**

- **Hilbert space** = complete inner-product space.
- **Self-adjoint operator:** $\langle T x, y \rangle = \langle x, T y
  \rangle$.
- **Spectral theorem:** every self-adjoint operator has a spectral
  decomposition.

Examples:

- Quantum observables: position $X$, momentum $P$, Hamiltonian $H$ —
  all self-adjoint on $L^2$.
- Multiplication and differentiation operators.

:::

> *Spectral theorem: symmetric/self-adjoint = orthogonal eigenbasis with real eigenvalues;*
> *SVD: every matrix has a decomposition (PCA, compression, denoising);*
> *Generalises to infinite dimensions: Hilbert spaces, quantum mechanics.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the SVD of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$.

Already diagonal with non-negative entries. **$U = I, \Sigma = A, V =
I$.** Trivial SVD.

For non-square or non-trivial: more work, but doable in closed form
for small matrices.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Symmetric: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Yes (entries above and below diagonal match).
</details>

**W2.** Diagonal of a positive-definite matrix?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
All entries $> 0$ (necessary, not sufficient).
</details>

**W3.** Singular values of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 5
\end{pmatrix}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5, 2$ (always sorted descending).
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Eigenvalues of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 4
\end{pmatrix}$? Verify orthogonal eigenvectors.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(4 - \lambda)^2 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 3, 5$. Eigenvectors
$(1, -1), (1, 1)$ — orthogonal ✓.
</details>

**P2.** Is $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$
positive semi-definite?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Eigenvalues: $\lambda^2 - \lambda - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = (1
\pm \sqrt{17})/2 \approx -1.56, 2.56$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No** — one negative eigenvalue.
</details>

**P3.** What does SVD compute that eigendecomposition can't?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Decomposition for *non-square* and *non-symmetric* matrices.**
Eigendecomposition only works for square matrices, and only nicely
(diagonalisable) for some. SVD is the universal generalisation.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Why is the SVD the foundation of modern data analysis?**

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**SVD reveals low-rank structure.** Most real-world data matrices are
approximately low-rank: top few singular values capture most of the
variance.

Applications:

- **PCA / dimensionality reduction.**
- **Latent semantic analysis** (NLP — find topics in documents).
- **Recommendation systems** (Netflix Prize, collaborative
  filtering).
- **Image compression.**
- **Computational physics** (model reduction).
- **Pseudoinverse** (handle non-invertible / over-determined
  systems).

The SVD's combination of universality (works on any matrix),
optimality (Eckart-Young), and computational efficiency makes it
*the* tool for matrix analysis.
</details>

**S2.** **Quantum mechanics in one sentence.** A quantum state is a
unit vector in a Hilbert space; observables are self-adjoint operators;
measurement of observable $O$ yields one of $O$'s eigenvalues, with
the post-measurement state being the corresponding eigenvector.

The full machinery of QM rests on the spectral theorem in Hilbert
space.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- **Particle position:** continuous spectrum on $\mathbb{R}$.
- **Energy of bound state:** discrete (quantised) spectrum.
- **Spin:** finite spectrum (e.g., $\pm 1/2$ for electrons).

Interference and uncertainty principles emerge from the *non-
commutativity* of observables: $XP - PX = i\hbar$.

The 20th-century triumph of mathematical physics: realising that
quantum phenomena require infinite-dimensional spectral theory.
Tier 7 (research track) develops this.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Spectral theorem only applies to symmetric matrices."** It
   applies to *normal* matrices ($A A^* = A^* A$), which include
   symmetric, Hermitian, unitary, orthogonal — many cases.
2. **"PCA is exotic."** It's just the spectral decomposition of the
   covariance matrix.
3. **"Infinite dim is too abstract."** Functional analysis is *the*
   language of quantum mechanics, signal processing, modern PDE
   theory.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **The [spectral theorem](/glossary.qmd#spectral-theorem) says
   every real symmetric matrix factors as $A = QDQ^T$** — orthogonal
   eigenbasis, real [eigenvalues](/glossary.qmd#eigenvalue), no
   funny business.
2. **The [SVD](/glossary.qmd#svd) generalises this to *any* matrix**
   — square or not, symmetric or not — and is the workhorse of
   PCA, recommender systems, and low-rank approximation.
3. **In infinite dimensions, the same theorem powers quantum
   mechanics** — observables are self-adjoint operators on a
   [Hilbert space](/glossary.qmd#hilbert-space), and their spectra
   are the values you can actually measure.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the spectral theorem for real symmetric matrices?
2. Without scrolling back, what's SVD for any matrix?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $A = U \Sigma V^T$ with orthogonal $U, V$ and diagonal
non-negative $\Sigma$.)

- **3 yes:** **You have completed Tier 6!** Tier 7 (Masters
  specialisation tracks: AI/ML, Game Dev, Encryption, Databases,
  Systems, Research) awaits.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (SVD applications).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P2 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T5 L04 — Eigenvalues](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)**
- **[T5 L15 — Real Analysis Foundation](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 7 — Functional Analysis** — full spectral theory in Hilbert
  space; bounded vs unbounded operators.
- **Tier 7 — Operator Algebras** — von Neumann algebras, C*-algebras.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** SVD-based decompositions everywhere
(S1). Spectral methods for graphs, kernels, embeddings.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Spectral theory unifies linear algebra
(finite), Fourier analysis (operators on $L^2$), and quantum
mechanics. Riemann hypothesis, Langlands program — all rely on
spectral ideas.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Physics.** Quantum mechanics (S2). Vibration analysis (eigenmodes
of structures). Quantum field theory uses operators on infinite-dim
spaces.
:::

---

## You have finished Tier 6

This lesson closes Tier 6 — Undergraduate Advanced.

Across 15 lessons you have:

1. Met **groups, rings, fields** and **group actions** — abstract
   algebra.
2. Worked with **complex numbers** and **complex analysis** through
   Cauchy's theorem.
3. Solved **second-order ODEs** and analysed the **classical PDEs**
   (heat, wave, Laplace).
4. Mastered **numerical methods** for roots, integrals, ODEs.
5. Learned **convex optimisation** and modern **gradient methods**
   (SGD, Adam).
6. Quantified information with **entropy, mutual information,
   channel capacity**.
7. Decomposed signals via **Fourier series** and **Fourier transform**.
8. Used **spectral graph theory** for clustering and partitioning.
9. Met the **spectral theorem** and a preview of **functional
   analysis**.

You are now ready for **Tier 7 — Masters Specialisation Tracks**
(planned), with parallel paths in:

- **Mathematical Research** (measure theory, functional analysis,
  topology).
- **AI / Machine Learning** (statistical learning theory, manifolds,
  RL).
- **Game Development** (differential geometry, quaternions,
  computational geometry).
- **Databases** (relational algebra, query optimisation, set
  theory).
- **Systems** (queueing theory, formal methods).
- **Encryption** (algebraic number theory, ECC, lattice crypto).

Congratulations.
